instruction: Reason about blameless postmortem culture: how to investigate incidents without creating fear and defensiveness.
output:
<think>
Blameless does not mean unaccountable. It means assuming good intentions and focusing on systemic causes rather than individual mistakes. The premise: intelligent, well-intentioned people made decisions that seemed reasonable given the information they had at the time. The system allowed the failure to happen. Postmortem structure: (1) Timeline of events with timestamps. (2) Impact (users affected, duration, revenue impact). (3) Root cause — the systemic reason, not "person X made a mistake." Ask: why did the system allow this failure? Why was there no automated check? Why was the rollback slow? (4) Contributing factors — what made the situation worse? (5) Action items with owners and deadlines. Rules: never name individuals in the report as responsible for the failure. Never punish people for honest mistakes. Do not skip postmortems because the fix was quick. Publish postmortems widely — learning from failures is the entire point.
</think>

instruction: Design psychologically safe code review practices that encourage learning and quality without creating adversarial dynamics.
output:
<think>
Psychologically safe code reviews follow specific principles: (1) Comment on code, not people — write "this function could be simplified" not "you wrote this wrong." (2) Ask questions instead of making demands — "what if we used a dict here for O(1) lookup?" not "use a dict." (3) Acknowledge good patterns — "nice use of the builder pattern here" builds trust. (4) Separate style from correctness — style disagreements should be resolved by linters, not humans. Remove opinion-based back-and-forth by automating formatting. (5) Explain the WHY behind suggestions — "this could cause a race condition because..." teaches, while "use a lock" just orders. (6) Use a checklist: does it work? Is it clear? Is it tested? Is it performant enough? (7) Set expectations: reviews should complete within one business day. Blocking PRs for days destroys flow. Culture principle: reviewing code is an act of helping your colleague ship better software, not gatekeeping or demonstrating superiority.
</think>

instruction: Analyze code ownership models: strong ownership vs collective ownership with tradeoffs and hybrid approaches.
output:
<think>
Strong ownership: one person or team owns each code area. They approve all changes, maintain quality standards, and have deep expertise. Pros: fast decisions within the owned area, deep expertise, clear accountability, consistent code style. Cons: bus factor (one person knows the code), bottlenecks (owner is on vacation), territorial behavior (resistance to changes from outside), knowledge silos. Collective ownership: anyone can change any code. Pros: flexibility (any developer can fix any bug), knowledge sharing (everyone learns everything), no bottlenecks. Cons: nobody feels responsible (tragedy of the commons), inconsistent patterns across the codebase, harder to maintain quality without clear ownership. Hybrid approach (recommended): team ownership. A team of 3-5 owns a bounded context. Any team member can approve changes. Teams follow shared conventions enforced by linters. Cross-team changes require approval from the owning team. Regular rotation of team members prevents silos.
</think>

instruction: Design a knowledge sharing system that prevents expertise silos as the engineering team grows.
output:
<think>
Knowledge silos are the biggest risk as teams scale. Mitigation mechanisms: (1) Pair programming — real-time knowledge transfer, rotate pairs regularly. (2) Code review rotation — do not always have the same person review the same area. Use round-robin assignment. (3) Tech talks — weekly 30-minute presentations where someone teaches something they learned. Low bar for quality, high bar for participation. (4) Architecture Decision Records — permanent written record of WHY decisions were made. New team members can read the history. (5) On-call rotation across services — forces engineers to learn systems outside their primary area. Include runbooks so on-call is not terrifying. (6) Internal documentation — not external-facing docs, but internal "how this system actually works" guides. (7) Shadowing — new team members shadow experienced engineers for their first on-call rotation. Measure: bus factor per component (how many people can maintain it independently). If any component has bus factor of 1, that is a critical risk. Target: minimum bus factor of 3 for all production systems.
</think>
